title: Overview
text: Tessera is a GPU kernel scheduler designed to multiplex many independent compute kernels onto a shared pool of GPU devices without requiring each kernel's author to manage placement manually. Rather than dedicating an entire GPU to a single job, Tessera can pack multiple smaller kernels onto the same device concurrently, provided their combined resource footprint fits within the device's limits. Kernels are submitted to Tessera as independent units of work carrying a declared memory footprint, an estimated runtime, and a priority tier. Tessera's scheduler uses these declarations to decide which kernels can safely run concurrently on a given device without exceeding its memory or compute capacity. This packing approach trades some scheduling overhead for substantially higher aggregate GPU utilization compared to dedicating whole devices to individual jobs. Tessera targets shared research-cluster and multi-tenant cloud deployments specifically, rather than the single-job, single-device pattern common in dedicated training runs.

title: Kernel launch model
text: A kernel submitted to Tessera passes through an admission queue before being dispatched to a specific GPU device. The admission queue evaluates each waiting kernel against the current occupancy of every available device, dispatching a kernel as soon as a device with sufficient free capacity becomes available. Kernels declared with a higher priority tier are considered for dispatch ahead of lower-priority kernels waiting in the same queue, though a sufficiently long wait eventually raises any kernel's effective priority. Once dispatched, a kernel runs to completion or until it exceeds a configured maximum runtime, after which Tessera forcibly terminates it and frees its reserved resources. This forced termination exists specifically to prevent a single misbehaving or mis-estimated kernel from indefinitely blocking capacity other queued kernels are waiting for.

title: Priority scheduling
text: Tessera assigns kernels to one of four priority tiers, with the highest tier reserved for latency-sensitive interactive workloads and the lowest for best-effort batch jobs. Within a tier, kernels are dispatched in roughly first-in-first-out order, though the admission queue may reorder slightly to better pack available device capacity. A kernel waiting significantly longer than its tier's expected wait time has its effective priority boosted temporarily, a mechanism intended to prevent starvation under sustained high load. This aging mechanism is deliberately conservative: it boosts priority gradually rather than immediately promoting a waiting kernel to the highest tier, to avoid destabilizing scheduling for genuinely latency-sensitive jobs. Operators can configure the aging rate per deployment, and clusters serving a mix of interactive and batch workloads typically use a more aggressive aging rate than single-purpose batch clusters.

title: Memory-aware admission control
text: Before dispatching a kernel, Tessera checks the kernel's declared memory footprint against the target device's currently available memory, accounting for every kernel already running on that device. A kernel whose declared footprint exceeds any device's available memory remains queued rather than being dispatched to a device where it would exceed capacity and likely fail. Declared footprints are advisory rather than enforced at the hardware level, so a kernel that significantly under-declares its actual memory usage can still cause an out-of-memory failure once running. Tessera's monitoring layer flags kernels whose actual memory usage substantially exceeds their declaration, since repeated under-declaration from the same source is usually a sign of a misconfigured job rather than a one-off mistake. Persistent under-declaration from a specific submitting team typically triggers a manual review before that team's future kernels are granted the same scheduling priority they previously received.

title: Multi-tenant isolation
text: Because Tessera packs kernels from potentially different teams onto the same physical device, isolating one tenant's kernel from interfering with another's is a central design concern. Memory isolation is enforced by the underlying GPU hardware's own memory protection, ensuring one kernel cannot read or write another kernel's allocated memory regardless of scheduling decisions. Compute isolation is weaker: kernels sharing a device do compete for the same compute units, and a poorly behaved kernel can meaningfully slow down co-scheduled kernels even without violating any memory boundary. Tessera partially mitigates compute interference by monitoring each kernel's actual runtime against its declaration and deprioritizing future submissions from sources that consistently interfere with co-scheduled kernels. Deployments with strict isolation requirements, such as workloads processing sensitive data from different customers, typically configure Tessera to avoid co-scheduling kernels from different tenants entirely, sacrificing utilization for stronger isolation.

title: Comparison to static partitioning
text: Static partitioning, the simpler alternative to Tessera's dynamic scheduling, dedicates a fixed subset of GPU devices to each team or workload class in advance. Static partitioning offers strong isolation and predictable performance by construction, since no two teams ever compete for the same device, but it typically leaves significant aggregate capacity idle whenever one team's allocation is under-utilized. Clusters that migrated from static partitioning to Tessera's dynamic scheduling have reported aggregate utilization improvements, though the exact improvement depends heavily on how correlated or anti-correlated different teams' workload patterns happen to be. Teams with highly bursty, unpredictable workloads benefit the most from dynamic scheduling, since static partitioning must otherwise provision for each team's peak demand independently, multiplying total required capacity. Tessera's designers generally recommend static partitioning only for workloads with unusually strict, non-negotiable isolation requirements that outweigh the utilization benefits dynamic scheduling otherwise provides.

title: Version history and capacity
text: Tessera 1.0 released in 2019 supported a maximum of 32 concurrent kernels per device and only two priority tiers. Tessera 2.0, released in 2021, expanded to four priority tiers and raised maximum concurrent kernels per device to 96. Tessera 2.4, released in 2022, introduced the aging mechanism described in the priority scheduling section. Tessera's current release supports a maximum of 128 concurrent kernels per device, the ceiling reached after further scheduler-overhead optimization work completed in 2023. A minor 2.4.2 patch later that year fixed an edge case in kernel-count accounting under rapid submission bursts.

title: GPU-accelerated compaction study reference (ALCB anchor)
text: Tessera has been used as the scheduling backend in several research studies exploring GPU-accelerated data processing, including a widely cited storage-engine compaction study. That study evaluated GPU-offloaded compaction against the Aurora LSM Compaction Benchmark, ALCB, whose reference dataset in the version used for the study totaled 1.8 terabytes of synthetic key-value data. The study reported a 3.4x compaction throughput improvement when merge-sort was offloaded to GPU hardware scheduled through Tessera, consistent with figures cited in follow-up storage-engine literature. Dataset size figures for ALCB have varied slightly across published studies, which the benchmark's maintainers attribute to periodic dataset regeneration between benchmark suite versions.

title: Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite (Zephyx anchor)
text: Tessera's evaluation methodology cross-validates certain timing measurements against the Zephyx scheduling benchmark suite, a benchmark originally developed for comparing real-time and best-effort scheduling algorithms broadly. The latest Zephyx suite version at the time of Tessera's most recent published evaluation was 4.4, which added a GPU-scheduling-specific workload profile absent from earlier suite versions. That workload profile models a mix of interactive and batch GPU kernels, closely matching the priority-tier structure Tessera itself implements. Tessera's designers note that Zephyx's GPU profile remains less widely adopted outside GPU-scheduling research specifically than the suite's original real-time-systems workloads.

title: Utilization by scheduling policy
text:
Aggregate GPU utilization varies substantially by scheduling policy, as measured across a representative shared research cluster. | Policy | Devices | Avg utilization | Peak utilization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static partitioning | 64 | 41% | 58% |
| Tessera dynamic | 64 | 73% | 91% | The utilization gap is largest for clusters serving workloads with weakly correlated demand across teams, exactly the scenario dynamic scheduling is designed to exploit. Utilization figures were collected over a rolling thirty-day window to smooth out day-to-day scheduling variance across the measured cluster.

title: Kernel submission checklist
text:
Teams submitting a new kernel type to a Tessera-managed cluster are expected to complete the following checklist. 1. Measure and declare an accurate memory footprint under representative input sizes.
2. Measure and declare an accurate expected runtime, including a reasonable safety margin.
3. Select the lowest priority tier consistent with the workload's actual latency requirements.
4. Test the kernel under co-scheduling with at least one other kernel type before production submission.
5. Register a contact for the monitoring layer to notify if declared and actual resource usage diverge significantly. Kernels that skip the co-scheduling test in step four are the most common source of unexpected compute-interference complaints after deployment.

title: International GPU scheduling research
text: GPU kernel scheduling for shared, multi-tenant clusters draws on scheduling-theory research published across several language traditions. Miyazaki and Sato's 複数テナント環境におけるGPUカーネルの動的スケジューリング (2017) proposes an early dynamic co-scheduling approach Tessera's admission queue design directly builds on. Egorova's Динамическое распределение ресурсов GPU в мультитенантных системах (2019) formalizes the memory-aware admission control model Tessera implements. Vasiliadis's study of δυναμική ανάθεση πόρων GPU (dynamic GPU resource allocation) provides the aging-mechanism analysis referenced in the priority scheduling section. Tessera's design documentation cites all three works directly in its scheduling-theory chapter.

title: Expected wait time under priority aging
text: Tessera's aging mechanism can be analyzed as a simple additive priority boost applied at fixed intervals to any kernel still waiting in the admission queue. Let p0 be a kernel's initial priority score and let the boost add a fixed increment d every interval of length T that the kernel remains unscheduled. After waiting for time w, a kernel's effective priority is p0 + d * floor(w / T), which grows without bound the longer the kernel waits, guaranteeing it eventually reaches the top of any finite queue. Choosing T too small causes priorities to shift so quickly that the tier structure itself becomes nearly meaningless under sustained load, while choosing T too large risks starvation for kernels waiting near, but under, the practical patience limit of their submitters. Tessera's default configuration sets T conservatively, favoring stability of the tier structure over faster starvation prevention, based on operational experience across several production clusters.

title: History: from static allocation scripts to a dedicated dynamic scheduler
text: Tessera began in 2018 as a set of internal allocation scripts that manually assigned specific GPU devices to specific jobs based on a spreadsheet maintained by a cluster administrator, with no dynamic scheduling logic whatsoever. This manual process worked adequately while the cluster hosted only a handful of teams with relatively stable, predictable workloads, but became increasingly unwieldy as more teams with bursty, unpredictable demand joined the same shared cluster. The administrator maintaining the allocation spreadsheet became a well-known bottleneck, since any team needing additional capacity had to wait for a manual spreadsheet update rather than being served automatically as capacity became available. The first automated version, built in 2019 as Tessera 1.0, replaced the spreadsheet with a simple first-in-first-out queue and a hard cap of 32 concurrent kernels per device, chosen conservatively based on early testing of how many kernels a device could reliably support without memory contention. That conservative cap left substantial capacity idle during periods when concurrent kernels used less memory than the cap assumed, a known limitation the original team accepted in exchange for a design simple enough to ship quickly. Feedback from early adopting teams consistently requested two things: more concurrent kernels per device, and some way to prioritize latency-sensitive interactive work over long-running batch jobs sharing the same queue. The 2.0 release addressed both requests simultaneously: memory-aware admission control allowed the concurrent kernel cap to rise substantially, since capacity was now determined by actual declared memory footprints rather than a fixed conservative number, and the four-tier priority system gave interactive workloads a defined path to faster scheduling. Raising the concurrent kernel cap this way was not merely a configuration change; it required building the memory-aware admission control logic essentially from scratch, since the original first-in-first-out queue had no concept of per-kernel resource declarations at all. The aging mechanism added in the 2.4 release directly addressed a starvation risk discovered under the new tiers, where a steady stream of high-priority interactive kernels could indefinitely delay lower-tier batch jobs, modeled explicitly on priority-aging techniques used in general-purpose operating system schedulers, adapted to the coarser granularity appropriate for GPU kernels running for seconds or minutes rather than microsecond-scale operating system tasks. Further scheduler-overhead optimization work through 2023 pushed the concurrent kernel ceiling from 96 to its current value of 128, primarily by reducing the per-kernel bookkeeping cost the admission queue's memory-aware checks had originally required. Looking back, the team identifies the transition from the manual spreadsheet to an automated first-in-first-out queue as the change that mattered most operationally, even though later changes, memory-aware admission and priority aging, delivered larger measured utilization and fairness improvements individually.

title: Summary: kernel packing behavior
text: This recap restates Tessera's core packing behavior for readers skimming only the overview section. Rather than dedicating an entire GPU to a single job, Tessera can pack multiple smaller jobs onto the same device concurrently, provided their combined resource footprint fits within the device's limits. The admission queue's exact dispatch logic is described in full in the kernel launch model section rather than repeated here. Nothing about this recap changes the underlying scheduling behavior, which remains as specified in the overview and kernel launch model sections. Teams evaluating Tessera for a new cluster should consult those sections directly rather than relying on this summary alone. This recap is regenerated automatically whenever the overview section's wording changes, to keep the two in sync.
